title: Influenza
oneLiner: Seasonal viral respiratory infection causing fever, cough, myalgia; can progress to severe pneumonia, ARDS, or secondary bacterial sepsis, especially in high-risk groups.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute respiratory failure (ARDS):** Rapidly falling SpO₂ (<90% on high-flow O₂), bilateral crepitations, increased work of breathing. Not simply "influenza with mild hypoxia."
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** Hypotension despite fluid resuscitation, rising lactate (>2 mmol/L), altered mental state – may be primary viral sepsis or secondary bacterial (e.g. Staph aureus, Strep pneumoniae).
- **Acute myocardial infarction / myocarditis:** Chest pain with widespread ST-elevation or troponin rise, plus new arrhythmia or heart failure in young adult. Influenza is a known trigger for MI.
- **Single finding that says "this is the killer":** Refractory hypoxia with bilateral infiltrates on CXR, combined with rapid deterioration in NEWS2 (≥7) within hours.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you'll actually see):**
  - Abrupt onset fever (>38°C), chills, rigors
  - Dry cough, sore throat, rhinorrhoea
  - Severe myalgia (especially back, legs), headache, malaise
  - Gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea) – more common in children
  - In elderly: atypical – confusion, falls, reduced mobility, or just hypothermia with hypoxia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Febrile, tachycardic, tachypnoeic
  - Pharyngeal erythema, conjunctival injection
  - Chest: clear or scattered wheeze/crackles (viral pneumonitis)
  - In severe disease: cyanosis, grunting, intercostal recession, use of accessory muscles
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Influenza** → fever+myalgia+cough, rapid onset
- **COVID-19** → anosmia/dysgeusia, longer incubation, can co-circulate – test both
- **RSV / adenovirus / parainfluenza** → more rhinorrhoea, wheeze in children; less myalgia
- **Bacterial pneumonia (e.g. Strep pneumoniae)** → productive purulent sputum, focal consolidation, raised CRP >100, procalcitonin high
- **Streptococcal pharyngitis** → exudative tonsillitis, tender cervical nodes, no cough
- **Sepsis from other source** → check urine, abdomen, skin for focus
- **Splitter:** Abrupt high fever with myalgia and dry cough → influenza (vs gradual onset with headache without myalgia → likely COVID)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Peripheral oxygen saturations – continuous monitoring if low
  - NEWS2 chart
  - Capillary blood gas if hypoxic or shocked
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - Blood cultures (if febrile/septic)
  - Troponin if chest pain or evidence of myocarditis
  - Influenza PCR (throat and nasal swab – combined swab in viral transport medium) – urgent, not always immediate result but informs cohorting
  - Consider: procalcitonin (differentiates bacterial from viral)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Chest X-ray (PA + lateral if able) – look for bilateral interstitial infiltrates, consolidation, or pneumothorax
  - Second-line: CT thorax if ARDS suspected or CXR equivocal
- **Special tests:**
  - Rapid antigen test (point-of-care) – low sensitivity; use PCR as gold standard
  - If admitted, consider extended respiratory viral panel (including COVID, RSV, adenovirus)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Clinical diagnosis typical in community surveillance (ILI = influenza-like illness: fever ≥38°C AND cough OR sore throat, no other cause)
  - Confirmed by PCR
- **Never-miss test:** Chest X-ray in all patients with respiratory distress or hypoxia to rule out bacterial co-infection; pulse oximetry – silent hypoxia can occur
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for high-risk groups (age ≥65, pregnancy, immunosuppressed, chronic resp/cardiac/renal disease, obesity BMI ≥40, children <5, any with new neuro symptoms)
  - Start O₂ if SpO₂ <94% – target 94–98%
  - Antivirals: **oseltamivir** (75 mg PO BD for 5 days) – ideally within 48h of onset; still offered in hospital if severe or in high-risk if >48h (check local policy)
  - Consider early antibiotics if evidence of bacterial co-infection (e.g. CURB-65 >2, bilateral shadows on CXR) – e.g. doxycycline or co-amoxiclav according to local guidelines
  - Sepsis treatment if shock – IV fluids, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics per Sepsis Six
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Infection prevention: single room with negative pressure if available, droplet precautions (surgical mask, gloves, apron), cohort with other influenza patients
  - Thromboembolic prophylaxis (VTE risk assessment; low molecular weight heparin if indicated)
  - Fluid balance – encourage oral fluids; IV 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann's if unable to maintain intake (e.g. 1 L/24h plus replacement of deficits)
  - Symptom control: paracetamol 1 g QDS for fever/myalgia; avoid NSAIDs if dehydration or renal risk
  - Monitor oxygen saturations, respiratory rate, NEWS2 at least 4-hourly
  - Consider oseltamivir continuation for 10 days if severe (e.g. ICU, immunocompromised) – check local policy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For low-risk, uncomplicated, sent home – rest, hydration, paracetamol, avoid work/school until 24h afebrile without antipyretics
  - Annual influenza vaccination – offer to high-risk groups, healthcare workers
  - Post-viral fatigue – gradual return to activity over weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl 1 L/24h [adjust based on clinical assessment]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20 mg/40 mg SC once daily (if high risk, normal renal function)
  - Diet/NBM: normal diet as tolerated; NBM only if severely unwell or imminent intubation
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours; if unstable → 1-2 hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) – 75 mg PO BD for 5 days (treatment); prophylaxis 75 mg PO OD for 10 days (only post-exposure in high-risk)
  - For severe/critical illness: may extend to 10 days (no strong evidence, but [check local policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Zanamivir (Relenza) – 10 mg inhaled BD for 5 days (if oseltamivir-resistant, or contraindicated; caution in asthma/COPD)
  - Baloxavir marboxil – not routinely used in UK/Ireland yet; [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oseltamivir – caution in severe renal impairment (eGFR <10 mL/min); reduce dose to 30 mg BD for treatment (see BNF)
  - Zanamivir – avoid if underlying chronic respiratory disease (bronchospasm risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Oseltamivir: eGFR <60 mL/min: dose adjustment needed – 75 mg BD if eGFR 30–60; 75 mg OD if eGFR 10–30; 30 mg OD if on haemodialysis. [Check Renal Drug Handbook / local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Omitting or delaying oseltamivir** in high-risk hospitalised patients – evidence shows benefit even beyond 48h; second common error is **prescribing prophylactic dose instead of treatment dose** (75 mg BD vs OD).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Viral pneumonia / secondary bacterial pneumonia (Staph aureus, Strep pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae)
  - Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
  - Sepsis / multiorgan failure
  - Myocarditis / pericarditis / acute coronary syndrome
  - Encephalitis / meningitis / Guillain-Barré syndrome
  - Rhabdomyolysis (severe myalgia with dark urine, high CK)
  - Febrile seizures (children)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-infectious fatigue syndrome (months)
  - Exacerbation of underlying COPD, asthma, heart failure
sections.Prognosis: - Majority recover within 1–2 weeks. Mortality <0.1% in healthy adults; up to 5–10% in hospitalised elderly or immunocompromised patients. Early antiviral therapy reduces severe outcomes. Mortality in ICU (ARDS) can be 30–40%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always swab for influenza PCR** before starting antivirals – but do not delay treatment pending result if high suspicion.
- **Prescribe oseltamivir** for all high-risk hospitalised patients, even if >48h from symptom onset – randomised trials show benefit in severe disease.
- **Check for bacterial co-infection** on day 3–5 if patient deteriorates (rising CRP, new consolidation) – cover with antibiotics.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use NSAIDs** for fever if dehydrated or with acute kidney injury – can worsen renal function and gastric irritation.
- **Do not send influenza patients home without ensuring they have clear safety netting** – hypoxia can be silent; if in doubt, request CXR before discharge.
- **Do not stop oseltamivir prematurely** in severe cases – 5-day course is standard, but if still requiring ICU, consider extending to 10 days (discuss with microbiology/infectious diseases).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, inability to keep down fluids, fever >5 days despite antipyretics, new rash, or fits.
- **Follow-up:** None needed in uncomplicated cases. If high-risk or on antivirals, advise GP review in 2–3 days to reassess.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – escalate to ward registrar/consultant for review within 30 min
- **SpO₂ <92% despite O₂ 4 L/min** – consider HDU/ICU for NIV or respiratory support
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to 30 mL/kg of IV fluid** – call ICU outreach for sepsis management
- **Reduced GCS (≤8) or seizure** – call crash team; consider encephalitis
- **Rising oxygen requirements, bilateral infiltrates on CXR** – discuss with ICU for early CPAP or intubation (avoid delay)

rawMarkdown:
## Influenza
**One-liner:** Seasonal viral respiratory infection causing fever, cough, myalgia; can progress to severe pneumonia, ARDS, or secondary bacterial sepsis, especially in high-risk groups.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute respiratory failure (ARDS):** Rapidly falling SpO₂ (<90% on high-flow O₂), bilateral crepitations, increased work of breathing. Not simply "influenza with mild hypoxia."
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** Hypotension despite fluid resuscitation, rising lactate (>2 mmol/L), altered mental state – may be primary viral sepsis or secondary bacterial (e.g. Staph aureus, Strep pneumoniae).
- **Acute myocardial infarction / myocarditis:** Chest pain with widespread ST-elevation or troponin rise, plus new arrhythmia or heart failure in young adult. Influenza is a known trigger for MI.
- **Single finding that says "this is the killer":** Refractory hypoxia with bilateral infiltrates on CXR, combined with rapid deterioration in NEWS2 (≥7) within hours.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you'll actually see):**
  - Abrupt onset fever (>38°C), chills, rigors
  - Dry cough, sore throat, rhinorrhoea
  - Severe myalgia (especially back, legs), headache, malaise
  - Gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea) – more common in children
  - In elderly: atypical – confusion, falls, reduced mobility, or just hypothermia with hypoxia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Febrile, tachycardic, tachypnoeic
  - Pharyngeal erythema, conjunctival injection
  - Chest: clear or scattered wheeze/crackles (viral pneumonitis)
  - In severe disease: cyanosis, grunting, intercostal recession, use of accessory muscles

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Influenza** → fever+myalgia+cough, rapid onset
- **COVID-19** → anosmia/dysgeusia, longer incubation, can co-circulate – test both
- **RSV / adenovirus / parainfluenza** → more rhinorrhoea, wheeze in children; less myalgia
- **Bacterial pneumonia (e.g. Strep pneumoniae)** → productive purulent sputum, focal consolidation, raised CRP >100, procalcitonin high
- **Streptococcal pharyngitis** → exudative tonsillitis, tender cervical nodes, no cough
- **Sepsis from other source** → check urine, abdomen, skin for focus
- **Splitter:** Abrupt high fever with myalgia and dry cough → influenza (vs gradual onset with headache without myalgia → likely COVID)

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Peripheral oxygen saturations – continuous monitoring if low
  - NEWS2 chart
  - Capillary blood gas if hypoxic or shocked
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - Blood cultures (if febrile/septic)
  - Troponin if chest pain or evidence of myocarditis
  - Influenza PCR (throat and nasal swab – combined swab in viral transport medium) – urgent, not always immediate result but informs cohorting
  - Consider: procalcitonin (differentiates bacterial from viral)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Chest X-ray (PA + lateral if able) – look for bilateral interstitial infiltrates, consolidation, or pneumothorax
  - Second-line: CT thorax if ARDS suspected or CXR equivocal
- **Special tests:**
  - Rapid antigen test (point-of-care) – low sensitivity; use PCR as gold standard
  - If admitted, consider extended respiratory viral panel (including COVID, RSV, adenovirus)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Clinical diagnosis typical in community surveillance (ILI = influenza-like illness: fever ≥38°C AND cough OR sore throat, no other cause)
  - Confirmed by PCR
- **Never-miss test:** Chest X-ray in all patients with respiratory distress or hypoxia to rule out bacterial co-infection; pulse oximetry – silent hypoxia can occur

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for high-risk groups (age ≥65, pregnancy, immunosuppressed, chronic resp/cardiac/renal disease, obesity BMI ≥40, children <5, any with new neuro symptoms)
  - Start O₂ if SpO₂ <94% – target 94–98%
  - Antivirals: **oseltamivir** (75 mg PO BD for 5 days) – ideally within 48h of onset; still offered in hospital if severe or in high-risk if >48h (check local policy)
  - Consider early antibiotics if evidence of bacterial co-infection (e.g. CURB-65 >2, bilateral shadows on CXR) – e.g. doxycycline or co-amoxiclav according to local guidelines
  - Sepsis treatment if shock – IV fluids, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics per Sepsis Six
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Infection prevention: single room with negative pressure if available, droplet precautions (surgical mask, gloves, apron), cohort with other influenza patients
  - Thromboembolic prophylaxis (VTE risk assessment; low molecular weight heparin if indicated)
  - Fluid balance – encourage oral fluids; IV 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann's if unable to maintain intake (e.g. 1 L/24h plus replacement of deficits)
  - Symptom control: paracetamol 1 g QDS for fever/myalgia; avoid NSAIDs if dehydration or renal risk
  - Monitor oxygen saturations, respiratory rate, NEWS2 at least 4-hourly
  - Consider oseltamivir continuation for 10 days if severe (e.g. ICU, immunocompromised) – check local policy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For low-risk, uncomplicated, sent home – rest, hydration, paracetamol, avoid work/school until 24h afebrile without antipyretics
  - Annual influenza vaccination – offer to high-risk groups, healthcare workers
  - Post-viral fatigue – gradual return to activity over weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl 1 L/24h [adjust based on clinical assessment]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20 mg/40 mg SC once daily (if high risk, normal renal function)
  - Diet/NBM: normal diet as tolerated; NBM only if severely unwell or imminent intubation
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours; if unstable → 1-2 hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) – 75 mg PO BD for 5 days (treatment); prophylaxis 75 mg PO OD for 10 days (only post-exposure in high-risk)
  - For severe/critical illness: may extend to 10 days (no strong evidence, but [check local policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Zanamivir (Relenza) – 10 mg inhaled BD for 5 days (if oseltamivir-resistant, or contraindicated; caution in asthma/COPD)
  - Baloxavir marboxil – not routinely used in UK/Ireland yet; [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oseltamivir – caution in severe renal impairment (eGFR <10 mL/min); reduce dose to 30 mg BD for treatment (see BNF)
  - Zanamivir – avoid if underlying chronic respiratory disease (bronchospasm risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Oseltamivir: eGFR <60 mL/min: dose adjustment needed – 75 mg BD if eGFR 30–60; 75 mg OD if eGFR 10–30; 30 mg OD if on haemodialysis. [Check Renal Drug Handbook / local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Omitting or delaying oseltamivir** in high-risk hospitalised patients – evidence shows benefit even beyond 48h; second common error is **prescribing prophylactic dose instead of treatment dose** (75 mg BD vs OD).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Viral pneumonia / secondary bacterial pneumonia (Staph aureus, Strep pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae)
  - Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
  - Sepsis / multiorgan failure
  - Myocarditis / pericarditis / acute coronary syndrome
  - Encephalitis / meningitis / Guillain-Barré syndrome
  - Rhabdomyolysis (severe myalgia with dark urine, high CK)
  - Febrile seizures (children)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-infectious fatigue syndrome (months)
  - Exacerbation of underlying COPD, asthma, heart failure

### Prognosis
- Majority recover within 1–2 weeks. Mortality <0.1% in healthy adults; up to 5–10% in hospitalised elderly or immunocompromised patients. Early antiviral therapy reduces severe outcomes. Mortality in ICU (ARDS) can be 30–40%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always swab for influenza PCR** before starting antivirals – but do not delay treatment pending result if high suspicion.
- **Prescribe oseltamivir** for all high-risk hospitalised patients, even if >48h from symptom onset – randomised trials show benefit in severe disease.
- **Check for bacterial co-infection** on day 3–5 if patient deteriorates (rising CRP, new consolidation) – cover with antibiotics.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use NSAIDs** for fever if dehydrated or with acute kidney injury – can worsen renal function and gastric irritation.
- **Do not send influenza patients home without ensuring they have clear safety netting** – hypoxia can be silent; if in doubt, request CXR before discharge.
- **Do not stop oseltamivir prematurely** in severe cases – 5-day course is standard, but if still requiring ICU, consider extending to 10 days (discuss with microbiology/infectious diseases).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, inability to keep down fluids, fever >5 days despite antipyretics, new rash, or fits.
- **Follow-up:** None needed in uncomplicated cases. If high-risk or on antivirals, advise GP review in 2–3 days to reassess.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – escalate to ward registrar/consultant for review within 30 min
- **SpO₂ <92% despite O₂ 4 L/min** – consider HDU/ICU for NIV or respiratory support
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to 30 mL/kg of IV fluid** – call ICU outreach for sepsis management
- **Reduced GCS (≤8) or seizure** – call crash team; consider encephalitis
- **Rising oxygen requirements, bilateral infiltrates on CXR** – discuss with ICU for early CPAP or intubation (avoid delay)
